subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear, logical progression linking historical motivation through algebraic model to conclusion
Well-structured report with title page, contents, section headings and bibliography
Concise, informative title and consistent referencing of figures and formulae
weaknesses: Occasional digressions (lengthy historical anecdotes, repeated proofs) interrupt flow
Table of contents entries misaligned and some page numbers missing
Inconsistent formatting in equations and bibliography entries
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is coherent and logically developed, with a clear organization and mostly relevant content. However, it contains unnecessary repetition and minor formatting lapses that prevent it from reaching the highest band. A score of 3/4 reflects solid structure and clarity with room for greater conciseness and polish.

strengths: Mostly consistent and accurate use of mathematical notation (e.g. bar e_{ij}, S_n polynomials, adjacency matrices)
Effective combination of algebraic expressions, induction tables, photographs and graph diagrams
Logical segmentation of proofs with bullet points and subheadings for clarity
weaknesses: Occasional notation slips (mixing \alpha_{ij} and a_{ij}, unexplained symbols)
Some figures lack captions or full context (e.g. missing loop counts)
Ambiguous recurrence notation and undefined terms (Ï_max, a_{i_uj_u})
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The communication demonstrates a good logical structure and clear presentation with effective use of multiple representations. Minor inconsistencies and undefined notation prevent an exemplary performance, placing it in the band 3 range (3/4).

strengths: Genuine personal narrative linking childhood domino games to the investigation
Original figures and re-derivation of constants show initiative
Independent thinking evident in adapting and extending known results
weaknesses: Approach closely follows existing templates without fully novel results
Creativity, while good, remains within expected boundaries of the topic
Could push further personal innovation in conjectures or alternative methods
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: There is clear evidence of personal engagement through narrative framing and original work, but the exploration does not yet reach exceptional originality or independence. A score of 2/3 reflects significant engagement with scope for deeper personal innovation.

strengths: Reflection is present throughout and includes some discussion of future extensions
Learnt outcomes and suggestions for directed graphs and complexity are identified
weaknesses: Analysis remains largely descriptive with limited probing of underlying assumptions
Critical evaluation of proof robustness and limitations is superficial
Opportunity for deeper insight into alternative strategies and combinatorial proofs is not fully exploited
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the student provides meaningful observations and future directions, the reflection lacks critical depth and sustained analysis. This places it closer to a basic reflective level (1/3) rather than a fully developed one.

strengths: Relevant mathematics (matrix algebra, permutations, graph theory) is used effectively
Derivations demonstrate thorough understanding with mostly correct results
Mathematics is fully commensurate with and well-applied at course level
weaknesses: Minor algebraic errors and typos in final formula denominators
Some steps rely on referenced results without full proof
Conceptual confusion in graph-theoretic statements (odd degree vs. circuits)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student applies relevant HL-level mathematics accurately and with good understanding. Minor slips and incomplete proofs prevent the work from reaching the top two bands. A score of 4/6 accurately reflects strong performance with room for greater rigor and precision.

strandSelections.reasoning: Arguments are generally presented in a logical order (define product â analyse properties â build polynomial â count trains).  Proofs are segmented with bullet points and subheadings, aiding clarity.  Occasional lapses (mis-typed base-case discussion, sudden symbol changes) prevent âexcellentâ clarity but the overall structure is good.

Derivations are mostly correct and the student demonstrates a solid grasp of why over-counting occurs.  Nonetheless, there are minor algebraic slips (typo in final formula denominator, unexplained symbols) and some steps rely on referenced results without full proof.  This indicates âgood understanding and mostly correctâ, matching band 4.

The student employs algebraic expressions, permutation sums, induction tables, domino photographs and graph diagrams.  These representations complement one another and help the reader visualise trains and Eulerian paths, showing **effective** though not outstanding use of multiple forms.

The exploration follows a clear story line: historical motivation â aim â preliminary observations â algebraic model â derivation â graph-theoretic interpretation â conclusion.  Ideas are linked in a sensible order and each new section builds on the previous one, so the reader can follow the mathematical argument without major jumps.  However, occasional digressions (long historical anecdotes, repeated explanations of the AÂ·B product, duplicated induction summaries) interrupt the flow and prevent the work from reaching the âhighly coherent/excellentâ standard.

Creating original figures, re-deriving the counting constant, and extending to Eulerian trails reflect **good creativity and initiative**.  Yet the exploration follows a known template and stops short of novel results, so it does not reach the âexceptionalâ level.

The student analyses results by linking vertex degrees to the feasibility of trains and by explaining over-counting factors.  The analysis is correct but relatively brief; limitations and robustness of proofs are only lightly touched upon.  Hence it is âthoughtfulâ but not âdeep and insightfulâ.

Outcomes are evaluated (formula applicability, over-counting, future research suggestions).  The evaluation recognises strengths and limitations but does not develop implications in detail.  This aligns with âclear evaluationâ.

The conclusion section discusses what was learned and mentions future extensions (complexity, directed graphs).  Reflection is present throughout but remains **descriptive**, seldom probing underlying assumptions or alternative strategies in depth, thus fitting âmeaningfulâ rather than âsubstantialâ reflection.

Symbols such as \(\bar e_{ij}\), \(S_n(X_1,\dots,X_n)\) and adjacency matrices are introduced and generally used correctly.  Notation slips occur (e.g., switching between \(\alpha_{ij}\) and \(a_{ij}\); unexplained âÎ³â in one formula), but they do not destroy meaning.  Therefore usage is âmostly consistentâ rather than flawless.

A personal narrative (childhood domino games, choice of topic) frames the investigation, and the decision to link matrices with graph theory shows a personal route through the material.  The approach remains largely within the scope of an existing paper, so it is âclearâ rather than âhighly personal/innovativeâ.

Most paragraphs are relevant to deriving the counting formula, yet the manuscript is 19 pages for a single core idea; historical background, duplicate proofs and repeated re-statements of results add bulk without advancing the mathematics.  Consequently the work is âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elementsâ rather than fully concise.

Matrix algebra, permutations, induction and Eulerian graph theory are all **relevant** to counting domino trains and are integrated effectively into the investigation.  The mathematics chosen is neither trivial nor superfluous, satisfying the âeffectiveâ descriptor.

The student extends ideas from Oller-MarcÃ©n by re-working proofs, adapting them to HL notation and adding their own inductive argument.  While not fully original, this demonstrates **significant evidence** of independent thinking beyond simple replication.

There is a title page, a numbered table of contents, section headings that match the contents, and a closing bibliography; figures and formulae are referenced.  This constitutes a **clear structure** typical of a well-organised report.  Minor weaknessesâpage numbers occasionally missing on images, some headings mis-alignedâkeep it from exemplary quality.

The topics (matrix units, symmetric polynomials, Eulerian paths) sit comfortably at HL level.  They are applied with reasonable rigourâproof by induction, discussion of commutativity/non-associativityâshowing mathematics âfully commensurate with course levelâ, though not at an advanced research level that would justify a 5 â 6 band.

annotations.comment: The table of contents exhibits a clear structure but page numbers and entries appear misaligned; adjust alignment to improve visual coherence.
The introduction offers engaging historical context, but the narrative could be condensed to focus more quickly on the mathematical problem.
The title is concise and clearly states the investigation focus, immediately orienting the reader to the mathematical aim.
The distinction between single and circular trains is well illustrated by self-created figures, enhancing reader comprehension.
Mn(â) and Sn(â) are introduced but their relationships could be clarified by specifying domain and codomain explicitly.
The induction conclusion on page 9 incorrectly states n=1+1=2; clarify that the proof establishes the result for all n by induction.
Footnotes include unexpected symbols (e.g. âÎ©â); verify OCR artifacts and ensure consistency in reference formatting.
The aim is articulated in personal terms and demonstrates genuine curiosity about the problem.
The derivation that each number appears n+2 times lacks a brief combinatorial justification; include a step-by-step count to reinforce clarity.
Figure 1 lacks a clear caption under the figure itself; label subfigures or add descriptive text to convey the forbidden placements.
The definition of \bar{e}_{ij} mixes inline and display notation; reformat to a clear standalone equation.
The product AÂ·B is defined but the nonassociative aspect refers to Ï_{max} without definition; either define Ï_{max} or remove it.
Commutativity should be demonstrated algebraically; include a short proof that AÂ·B = BÂ·A for matrix units.
The induction list on page 9 omits justification of the base case reasoning; explicitly state why \bar e_{i_1j_1}â 0 ensures the proposition.
The argument that each new domino has two placement options would benefit from a concise combinatorial proof.
Link the algebraic polynomial S_n explicitly to the product AÂ·B structure to make the combinatorial connection more rigorous.
In the recurrence c(n)=2*c(n 1) the notation âc(n 1)â is ambiguous; correct to c(nâ1) for clarity.
Divide Î±_{ij} by 2^{nâ1} to correct overcounting, but include an explicit derivation of that factor from bidirectional placements.
The general formulaâs final display lacks definition of a_{i_uj_u}; restate variables clearly.
The recursion for Î± in the HL step is dense; add more intermediate algebraic steps to guide the reader.
The graph definition reads âG = V,Eâ but the conventional notation is G=(V,E); revise for formal accuracy.
Figure 3 is a strong original graphic but could note the number of vertices and loops for fuller context.
State explicitly that a graph with exactly two vertices of odd degree yields only trails, not circuits, to avoid conceptual confusion.
The claim on page 14 that a trail implies a circuit is incorrect; clarify that only all-even-degree graphs yield circuits.
Eulerian Circuit formula uses denominator 2n while earlier overcounting used 2^{nâ1}; reconcile the two and explain the factor change.
The concluding evaluation describes achievements but lacks critical discussion of limitations; reflect more on proof robustness and assumptions.
The suggestion to explore algorithms and approximations for S_n coefficients shows genuine foresight for future work.
Bibliography entries vary in style; adopt a consistent citation format for authors, titles, and dates throughout.
